stem: In a legal outcomes study comparing Judge A vs. Judge B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher acquittal rate (64.2% vs 59.1%). However, when stratified by case severity: In misdemeanors (n_X=30, n_Y=80): Treatment X achieves 81% vs Treatment Y's 75%. In felonies (n_X=80, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 51% vs Treatment Y's 43%. The true population proportion of misdemeanors is uncertain, estimated between 35% and 46%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to felonies (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.071, 0.073], point estimate: 0.072. Treatment X is genuinely better by 7.2 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=59.1%, Y=64.2% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In misdemeanors: X=81% > Y=75%. In felonies: X=51% > Y=43%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Judge A is applied more to felonies (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
